bullets.ppt_text: •Model capacity and data capacity should be matched to prevent overfitting and underfitting.
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explained that model capacity refers to the ability to approximate a wide variety of functions — a high-capacity model can fit many complex scenarios, while a low-capacity model (like linear regression) struggles with non-linear patterns. The classic analogy given was fitting a quadratic curve (y = x² + noise): a linear model underfits because it can't capture the true shape, while a very high-degree polynomial overfits by memorizing every data point including noise. The teacher emphasized that if you have a small dataset you need a simple (low-capacity) model, and if your data is complex you need a high-capacity model — mismatching the two in either direction leads to underfitting or overfitting. Getting this balance right is foundational to building a model that generalizes well to unseen data.

bullets.ppt_text: •Understand fitting and bias/variance relationship •Empirically, use training/validation error to evaluate •Know what to do under whichcircumstance
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explained the bias/variance trade-off through loss curves: high bias (underfitting) shows as a flat loss curve that barely decreases over epochs, while high variance (overfitting) shows as a large gap between training loss (steadily decreasing) and validation loss (plateauing or rising). Empirically, you read your progress board (loss curves) to diagnose which problem you have — the desired case is a small but inevitable gap between training and validation loss, both decreasing steadily. For underfitting, the remedies are to train longer, use a bigger/more complex neural network, or change the architecture; for overfitting, the remedies are to add more data, do architecture search, or apply regularization techniques. The teacher stressed that in real projects it's common to face both high bias and high variance simultaneously, so the practical goal is to at least reach a state of pure overfitting before tackling the gap.
